text: This is a video presentation of laparoscopic supra-elevator posterior accentuation in a case of previous laparoscopic ovarian transposition. Our patient is a twenty eight year old lady with locally advanced rectal cancer with biopsy suggestive of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with MRI suggestive of involvement of the anterior peritoneal reflection. The patient is planned for neoadjuvant chemoradiation prior to which she underwent laparoscopic ovarian transposition. The MRI done post neoadjuvant chemo radiation shows persistent involvement of the anterior peritoneal reflection and the patient is planned for laparoscopic supra levator posterior accentration.

The surgeon positions the standard laparoscopic ports for rectal surgery. Dissection begins by creating a plane below the inferior mesenteric vein, proceeding medially to laterally along the mesocolic fascia. The inferior mesenteric vein is clipped and divided near the DJ flexure.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the sigmoid colon using a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating adhesions formed due to previous laparoscopic ovarian transposition.

The surgeon retracts the fully mobilized sigmoid colon ventrally and incises the medial peritoneal fold. Dissection proceeds in the avascular plane below the inferior mesenteric artery, extending from medial to lateral and inferiorly into the pelvis posteriorly.

The surgeon bares the inferior mesenteric artery at its root, performing high ligation, clipping, and division. The dissection continues from medial to lateral along the mesocolic fascia, reaching the lateral peritoneal attachment. The lateral peritoneal cut is made, and the descending colon and splenic flexure are mobilized.

The surgeon scores the pelvic peritoneum along the iliac vessels, reaching the round ligament, which is dissected, clipped, and divided. The pelvic dissection continues to create the para-rectal and para-bicycle spaces, followed by the dissection of the left ureter, proceeding towards the UV junction.

The surgeon continues the ureteric dissection towards the uretero-vesical junction, encountering the uterine artery. The uterine artery, which separates the para-rectal and para-vesical spaces, is clipped and divided. The dissection then proceeds along the ureter until reaching the uretero-vesical junction.

The surgeon incises the uterovesical fold of peritoneum, separating the bladder from the lower uterine segment and cervix. The same procedure is performed on the opposite side, ensuring symmetrical dissection.

The surgeon begins dissecting the ureter near the pelvic inlet. The round ligament is dissected, clipped, and divided. The dissection of the ureter continues further into the pelvis until the uterine artery is identified.
The surgeon clips and divides the opposite uterine artery, followed by posterior dissection towards the pelvic floor, revealing the anatomical structures and spaces within the pelvis.

The surgeon cuts the antevaginal wall using a monopolar cautery to open the vaginal vault, followed by the lateral dissection of the rectum. An arrow highlights the hypogastric nerve, indicating its preservation during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection of the rectum down to the pelvic floor, then incises the posterior vaginal wall to finalize the vaginal cut. Following this, the dissection proceeds in the rectovaginal septum to fully mobilize the rectum.
The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision by fully mobilizing the rectum down to the pelvic floor. An endostepler is then used to divide the distal end of the rectal specimen.
The surgeon sutures the vaginal vault using a V-lock suture, closing the previously opened area.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a circular stapler, aligning the colon correctly before firing the stapler to secure the connection.
The surgeon inserts Jackson-Pratt drains into the pelvis and performs a covering ileostomy.

The final list of pathology revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with negative proximal and distal resection margins with negative circumferential resection margin and three out of fifteen lymph nodes positive
